source_files: Elicit - Gut Microbiome and Health A Bidirectional Review - Report.pdf

findings.title: Bidirectional Communication via Bile Acids and Microbial Metabolites
findings.summary: The gut microbiome exerts systemic effects through the hepatic axis, where hepatic secretions shape intestinal microbial ecology while microbial metabolites influence hepatic gene expression and metabolism. Microbial metabolites including butyrate and propionate serve as epigenetic modifiers affecting hepatic gene expression rhythmicity. Dysregulation of this axis contributes to alcoholic liver disease, non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NAFLD), liver cirrhosis, cholangitis, and hepatocellular carcinoma.
mechanism: Bile acid circulation influences microbial composition and metabolism
Microbial production of butyrate and propionate affects hepatic gene expression
Dysbiosis impairs hepatic glucose metabolism and increases fatty liver risk
Bacterial metabolites regulate cytochrome P450-mediated xenobiotic metabolism
clinical_outcomes: NAFLD/fatty liver disease
Cirrhosis
Altered liver function
Metabolic dysfunction
Reduced bile acid metabolism

findings.title: Neural, Endocrine, Immune, and Metabolic Signaling
findings.summary: The gut-brain axis mediates bidirectional communication through neural, endocrine, immune, and metabolic signaling pathways. Gut bacteria produce neuroactive molecules including GABA, serotonin precursors, and dopamine that engage the enteric nervous system and potentially cross the blood-brain barrier via vagal afferent signaling. Dysbiosis correlates with neuropsychiatric conditions through mechanisms including impaired blood-brain barrier permeability, altered neurotransmitter production, and systemic inflammation affecting neurological function.
mechanism: Vagus nerve-mediated neural communication between gut and central nervous system
Bacterial production of neurotransmitters: GABA, serotonin precursors, dopamine
Dysbiosis impairs blood-brain barrier integrity through reduced short-chain fatty acid production
Altered neuroimmune signaling and systemic inflammation affecting neurological function
clinical_outcomes: Improved cognitive function with probiotic supplementation
Reduced inflammatory markers in Alzheimer's disease patients
Inverse correlations with anxiety and depression
Altered neurotransmitter production in dysbiosis
Neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration

findings.title: Continuous Mucosal Crosstalk Affecting Systemic Immunity
findings.summary: The gut-immune axis involves continuous molecular crosstalk affecting both mucosal and systemic immunity. Gut microbiota shape immune system development and function through multiple mechanisms: commensal bacteria induce tolerogenic inflammatory regulatory T cells (Treg) while others promote pro-inflammatory Th17 cells. Short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) promoted regulatory T cell differentiation and modulated cytokine production. In autoimmune conditions, dysbiosis promoted Th17 cell differentiation, influenced regulatory T-cell balance, and triggered autobody production through molecular mimicry and pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs).
mechanism: Commensal bacteria induce tolerogenic anti-inflammatory Treg cells
Dysbiotic bacteria promote pro-inflammatory Th17 cell differentiation
Short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, propionate) promote Treg differentiation
Microbial metabolites regulate mucosal barrier function and antimicrobial peptide secretion
Dysbiosis triggers pathogenic immune responses and autoantibody production
clinical_outcomes: Dysbiosis promotes Th17 cell differentiation in autoimmune conditions
Reduced mucosal immunity in dysbiotic states
Increased systemic inflammation and immune dysregulation
Altered intestinal epithelial barrier function
IBD remission via dysbiosis intervention

findings.title: Immune Modulation and Short-Chain Fatty Acid Production
findings.summary: The gut-skin axis connects intestinal microbial balance with dermatological health outcomes. Gut dysbiosis associates with psoriasis, atopic dermatitis, acne vulgaris, rosacea, alopecia areata, and hidradenitis suppurativa. Mechanisms involve immune dysregulation, altered metabolic profiles, and reduced short-chain fatty acid production. Bifidobacterium infantis supplementation reduced plasma levels of inflammatory cytokines TNF-alpha, IL-6, and CRP with associated improvement in psoriasis symptoms. The gut-skin connection operates through immune modulation and production of short-chain fatty acids that maintained gut barrier function and reduced systemic inflammation affecting skin homeostasis.
mechanism: Dysbiosis impairs short-chain fatty acid production affecting barrier integrity
Increased systemic inflammation spreads from dysbiotic gut to skin
Altered immune response (Th17 promotion) affects skin inflammation
Dysbiosis-associated increased intestinal permeability elevates systemic LPS and inflammatory markers
Reduced antimicrobial peptide secretion increases pathogenic skin colonization
clinical_outcomes: Psoriasis symptom improvement with probiotic supplementation
Acne vulgaris associated with dysbiosis
Atopic dermatitis linked to dysbiotic microbiota
Reduced inflammatory cytokines in skin with improved microbiota
Hair loss (alopecia) associated with dysbiosis
Rosacea flares correlate with gut dysbiosis

findings.title: Microbial Composition and Respiratory Outcomes
findings.summary: The gut-lung axis links respiratory disorders with intestinal microbial composition, with airway symptoms among the most common extra-intestinal manifestations of IBD. Individual bacterial families either increased or decreased lung disease risk through systemic immune modulation. Dysbiosis shows preliminary associations between dysbiosis and tract conditions, though mechanisms remain less characterized than other organ axes.
mechanism: Dysbiosis-driven systemic inflammation spreads to respiratory mucosa
Altered immune tolerance in dysbiotic states increases airway inflammation
Reduced short-chain fatty acid production impairs respiratory barrier function
Dysbiosis-associated increased intestinal permeability elevates systemic LPS exposure
clinical_outcomes: Dysbiosis associated with asthma and allergic airway disease
IBD patients with respiratory symptoms and dysbiosis
COPD exacerbation risk linked to dysbiosis
Altered immune tolerance in dysbiotic states
Respiratory infection susceptibility in dysbiosis

findings.title: Dysbiosis and Urinary Tract Dysfunction
findings.summary: The gut-renal and gut-bladder axes remain less characterized than other organ systems, showing preliminary associations between dysbiosis and urinary tract conditions. Evidence remains preliminary with limited mechanistic understanding compared to the hepatic, neural, immune, and cutaneous axes.
mechanism: Dysbiosis-associated metabolite dysregulation affects urinary composition
Altered systemic inflammation may impact renal function
Dysbiosis increases pathogenic bacterial translocation to urinary tract
Dysbiosis-driven changes in urinary metabolite profiles promote UTI risk
clinical_outcomes: Preliminary associations between dysbiosis and urinary tract infections
Dysbiosis may impair kidney function through systemic inflammation
Altered urinary metabolite profiles in dysbiosis
Limited mechanistic understanding compared to other axes

findings.title: Beta-Glucuronidase-Mediated Estrogen Conjugation
findings.summary: The estrogen-gut microbiome axis revealed that gut bacteria regulate circulating estrogen levels through beta-glucuronidase secretion. Dysbiosis impairs estrogen deconjugation and recirculation, contributing to metabolic, reproductive, cardiovascular, and cognitive conditions in post-menopausal women. The estrobolome (estrogen-metabolizing microbial genes) mediates estrogen metabolism, reabsorption, and systemic bioavailability. Dysbiosis reduces beta-glucuronidase activity, lowering circulating estrogen and contributing to menopausal symptom severity and post-menopausal health complications.
mechanism: Microbial beta-glucuronidase deconjugates estrogen metabolites for reabsorption
Dysbiosis reduces beta-glucuronidase activity, lowering circulating estrogen
Dysbiosis impairs estrogen entero-hepatic circulation
Dysbiosis-associated dysregulation affects estrogen-dependent metabolic processes
Reduced estrogen bioavailability increases menopausal symptoms in dysbiotic women
clinical_outcomes: Dysbiosis impairs estrogen metabolism and increases menopausal symptoms
Reduced circulating estrogen in dysbiotic post-menopausal women
Dysbiosis contributes to metabolic dysfunction in post-menopause
Dysbiosis increases cardiovascular risk in post-menopausal women
Dysbiosis-driven cognitive decline in estrogen-deficient states
